data.source: Controlled laboratory experiment (subjects recruited via ORSEE; sessions implemented using z-Tree)
data.wave_or_subset: Not applicable; between-subjects design. Total sample reported: 320 subjects, with 124 in the main treatments and 100 in the one-stage-lottery (OSL) treatment (as reported).
data.sample_size: 320 subjects total (124 in main treatments; 100 in OSL treatment reported in paper summary).
data.notes: Between-subjects, one-shot experimental design. Parameterization chosen to set theoretical equilibrium effort at 74 points. Data collection implemented via z-Tree. Recruitment via ORSEE. No dataset links or data access procedures reported in the provided summary.

method.description: The study develops a simple theoretical model of multistage elimination tournaments (four identical agents, outputs depend on effort plus a uniform noise term) and derives symmetric subgame perfect Nash equilibrium effort levels via backward induction. It then tests the predictions experimentally in a between-subjects laboratory design implemented with z-Tree, comparing one-stage (OS), two-stage (TS), three-stage (THS), and a one-stage-lottery (OSL) control treatment. Subjects choose effort (scaled to points) and are paid according to tournament outcomes; personal characteristics (competitiveness, risk attitudes, overconfidence, cognitive ability, forward-looking behavior) were measured to analyze heterogeneity.
steps: Derive theoretical equilibrium efforts via backward induction for multistage tournament model (noise uniformly distributed on [-q,q], quadratic effort costs).
Compute equilibrium effort targets (parameters set so equilibrium predicted effort = 74).

Design experimental treatments: OS (one-stage), TS (two-stage elimination), THS (three-stage), OSL (one-stage lottery control), and relevant payoff structures to ensure strategic equivalence where intended.
Recruit subjects using ORSEE and run between-subjects sessions implemented in z-Tree.
Record chosen effort levels for each subject and stage, along with treatments and measured personal characteristics.

Analyze data by comparing mean/median effort levels to theoretical predictions and across treatments using nonparametric tests (Mann-Whitney U-tests, Wilcoxon signed-rank tests) and t-tests where appropriate; analyze heterogeneity via regressions or subgroup comparisons (as reported).
method.models: Theoretical symmetric subgame perfect Nash equilibrium model for multistage elimination tournaments with outputs = effort + uniform noise term. Equilibrium expressions provided for stage efforts, e.g., e_{i,2}^{TS} = ((w_high - w_med) c) / (4q) and e_{i,1}^{TS} = ((EV_{i,2}^{TS} - w_low) c) / (4q). Empirical analysis uses nonparametric tests (Mann-Whitney U, Wilcoxon signed-rank) and t-tests; heterogeneity analyses relate effort to measured personal characteristics (likely via regression or subgroup comparisons, exact regression specifications not provided in summary).
method.independent_variables: Treatment indicator (OS, TS, THS, OSL), stage (first, second, final), measured personal characteristics (competitiveness, risk attitudes, overconfidence, cognitive ability, forward-looking behavior)
method.control_variables: Personal characteristic measures used in heterogeneity analysis (competitiveness, risk attitudes, overconfidence, cognitive abilities, forward-looking behavior); exact control set for statistical models not stated in summary.
method.tools_software: z-Tree (experimental implementation); ORSEE (recruitment). Specific statistical software/packages for analysis not stated in summary.

results.summary: Experimental results show that effort in the one-stage tournament (OS) closely matched the theoretical Nash equilibrium (predicted 74; observed mean ~71.7). In the two-stage tournament (TS), subjects exerted substantially higher effort in the first stage (mean 84.8), significantly above both the theoretical prediction and the OS mean. Effort in the TS second stage fell to about 76.7, close to the predicted 74 and not significantly different from OS efforts. In the three-stage tournament (THS), subjects exerted above-equilibrium effort in early stages (79.2 and 83.1) and near-equilibrium effort in the final (74.6). The one-stage lottery control (OSL) produced average effort between OS and TS (77.3), not significantly different from equilibrium. Statistical tests reported: Mann-Whitney U-tests, Wilcoxon signed-rank tests, and t-tests, with several comparisons reaching statistical significance (e.g., TS first-stage > OS, p=0.011). Heterogeneity analyses found limited explanatory power of measured personal characteristics for excess effort in TS, although competitiveness increased effort more in TS than OS for some comparisons.

metadata.original_paper_title: Promotions and Incentives: The Case of Multistage Elimination Tournaments
